Tokenizers split text into subword units averaging 3-4 characters each in English; a 750-word document is approximately 1,000 tokens. Understanding tokenization is critical for cost estimation, context window planning, and avoiding truncation errors
Tokenization only affects model training, not API inference — developers do not need to understand it for production use
One word always equals exactly one token in all modern language models
explain: Tokenizers (such as BPE used by Claude models) split text into subword units rather than words or characters. In English, one token averages 3-4 characters, so 1,000 tokens is approximately 750 words. This matters because Bedrock charges per input and output token, context window limits are measured in tokens, and different languages tokenize differently (CJK languages require more tokens per word than English). Developers must estimate token counts accurately to avoid unexpected costs and context window truncation.

q: An application sends a prompt to Amazon Bedrock that exceeds the foundation model's maximum context window. What happens and how should this be handled?
options: The model automatically truncates the prompt from the end and processes what fits, producing a partial but successful response
The API returns a validation error; handle this by chunking long inputs, using RAG to retrieve only relevant passages, or summarizing earlier conversation turns to stay within the token budget
The model processes the entire input regardless of context window limits by internally paging through the document
All Amazon Bedrock models have unlimited context windows and never reject prompts based on length
explain: When a prompt exceeds the model's context window, Amazon Bedrock returns a validation error rather than silently truncating. The correct prevention strategy is to manage token budgets proactively: use RAG to retrieve only the 3-5 most relevant passages instead of the full document; summarize earlier conversation turns to compress history; chunk large documents and process them sequentially. The context window is a hard limit that must be respected by application design.

q: A company wants to establish an AI governance framework for their Amazon Bedrock deployments across multiple teams. Which components are essential?
options: Use policies, audit logs, access controls, and regular reviews covering model usage, data handling, and incident response
Governance is not needed for managed AWS services — Amazon handles all compliance requirements automatically
Allow unrestricted model access to all developers — governance slows down AI innovation unnecessarily
Governance only applies to on-premises AI systems; cloud-managed services like Bedrock have no governance requirements
explain: An effective AI governance framework for Amazon Bedrock deployments requires: acceptable use policies defining what data can be sent to foundation models; IAM access controls restricting which teams can invoke which models; Bedrock invocation logging to S3 for audit trails; model cards documenting each deployment's intended use and limitations; a review process for high-risk applications; and regular bias and safety audits. AWS manages infrastructure compliance, but application-level governance is the customer's responsibility under the shared responsibility model.

q: A developer needs to estimate the token count of prompts before sending them to Amazon Bedrock to manage costs and avoid context window errors. Which approach is most practical?
options: Use the model provider's tokenizer library (e.g., Anthropic's tokenizer for Claude models) to count tokens locally before making the API call
No estimation is needed — Bedrock automatically handles token counting and billing without developer awareness
Count the number of words and divide by 0.75 as a rough approximation — this is always accurate enough for production use
Always send the full prompt and rely on API error responses to detect when the limit is exceeded
explain: The most accurate approach is to use the same tokenizer the model uses locally before making the API call. Anthropic provides a tokenizer for Claude models. This allows developers to validate token counts, split oversized inputs, and estimate costs without incurring API calls. A rough word-based estimate (words divided by 0.75) works for quick estimates but varies significantly for code, non-English text, and special characters. Relying on API errors for detection wastes API calls and may cause runtime failures.

q: A healthcare company is deploying a generative AI application on Amazon Bedrock in a regulated environment. What compliance requirements must be addressed?
options: Regulated industries have no additional AI compliance requirements beyond standard AWS security controls
Ignore regulations until they are formally enforced — compliance requirements for AI are too new to be strictly enforced

Data privacy (HIPAA for health data), audit trails of all model interactions, explainability for clinical decisions, human oversight for high-stakes outputs, and documented result verification processes
Compliance is handled entirely by Amazon Bedrock's managed service — no additional customer configuration is required
explain: Regulated healthcare AI deployments must address multiple compliance layers: HIPAA requires a BAA with AWS; PHI must never leave approved data paths; access controls must be auditable. Audit trails of all model inputs and outputs satisfy HIPAA documentation requirements. Clinical AI outputs require explainability mechanisms so clinicians understand why a suggestion was made. Human-in-the-loop review is required for high-stakes decisions. Result verification ensures AI-generated content is validated before influencing patient care.

q: A team is preparing training data for fine-tuning a foundation model on Amazon Bedrock. How should the training dataset be structured for best results?
options: Organized in JSONL format with clear input-output pairs, consistent formatting matching production prompts, diverse coverage of the target task, and free of systematic labeling errors
Training data should be in random order with mixed formats to force the model to learn robust representations
Document quantity is the only factor that matters — collect as many examples as possible regardless of quality
Fine-tuning does not require structured data — raw text dumps are sufficient for all fine-tuning scenarios
explain: Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning requires JSONL format (one training example per line) with each example containing a prompt and expected completion. The format must match what the model will see during production inference. Data quality outweighs quantity: 1,000 high-quality, correctly labeled examples typically outperform 10,000 noisy ones. Diversity across the target task distribution prevents overfitting to common examples. Consistent formatting ensures the model learns the task pattern, not artifacts of inconsistent input structure.

q: A production fine-tuned model's performance degrades over time as user queries evolve. How should teams prevent and manage distribution shift between training and production data?
options: Monitor input query distributions for drift using statistical tests, maintain data quality pipelines, and periodically retrain or fine-tune on recent production data to keep the model current
Distribution shift cannot be prevented or managed once a model is deployed
Increase model size — larger models are immune to distribution shift
Deploy multiple model versions simultaneously and randomly route traffic to average out distribution shift effects
explain: Distribution shift occurs when the statistical properties of production queries diverge from training data, degrading model performance on patterns it was not adequately trained on. Prevention requires: monitoring incoming query distributions for drift using population stability index or KL divergence metrics; evaluating model accuracy on a rolling validation set; maintaining a feedback pipeline that flags degraded outputs; and periodically fine-tuning on recent production data that reflects current query patterns. The cadence depends on how quickly the domain evolves.

q: A team is building a multilingual application using Amazon Bedrock. Why do different languages require different numbers of tokens to represent equivalent content?
options: All languages use exactly the same number of tokens for equivalent meaning — tokenizers are language-agnostic
Token count differences between languages are negligible and can be ignored for cost planning
Only English has a defined tokenization standard; other languages use character-level encoding with predictable token counts

Tokenizers are trained primarily on English text, so subword vocabularies are English-optimized. CJK languages (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) often require 2-3x more tokens than English for equivalent meaning, significantly affecting API costs and context window usage
explain: BPE and SentencePiece tokenizers encode common English subwords as single tokens, but for languages with different scripts or less training data representation, each character may map to multiple tokens. Chinese text can require 2-3x the token count of equivalent English text. This has direct cost implications (token-based billing) and context window implications (shorter effective context for non-English content). Multilingual application teams must account for this in their token budget planning.

q: A developer is choosing between foundation models with different context window sizes for a document analysis application. How do larger context windows expand the types of problems a model can solve?
options: Larger context windows allow processing entire books, full codebases, and extended multi-turn conversations without chunking or summarization, enabling tasks impossible with smaller windows
Context window size has no effect on problem complexity — all models handle documents of any length equally
Larger context windows only improve performance on image processing tasks, not text analysis
Context window size only affects the maximum response length, not how much input can be processed
explain: Context window size is a fundamental capability constraint. Models with 4K token windows cannot process a 50-page document in a single call without chunking. Models with 200K token windows (like Claude 3) can analyze entire legal contracts, codebases, or multi-document research corpora in a single request, enabling tasks like cross-document synthesis, full-book summarization, and extended agent conversations. This eliminates the complexity and quality loss associated with chunking strategies, though inference cost scales with context length.

q: A team deploys an Amazon Bedrock-powered application and wants to continuously improve it based on user behavior. What feedback mechanisms should be implemented?
options: No feedback mechanisms are needed — foundation models self-improve through ongoing inference without explicit feedback
Feedback collection is only relevant during model training, not production deployment
Only A/B testing of different prompts is needed — user ratings introduce bias into the feedback signal

Implement explicit user ratings (thumbs up/down), implicit behavioral signals (edits to AI output, follow-up clarifying questions), usage analytics (which features are used most), and error logging — together these identify quality issues and prioritize improvements
explain: Multi-modal feedback collection creates a continuous improvement loop. Explicit ratings directly signal response quality. Implicit signals (users editing AI-generated content, requesting regeneration, asking clarifying questions) reveal quality gaps without requiring user effort. Usage analytics show which features deliver value and which are abandoned. Error logs capture failures before users report them. Together, these data sources guide prompt engineering improvements, fine-tuning data curation, and guardrail configuration.

q: A developer is building a multi-turn customer support chatbot using Amazon Bedrock's Converse API. How should conversation history be managed to maintain coherence while respecting context window limits?
options: Truncate conversation history at random to stay within token limits — users rarely reference early conversation turns
Always include the complete conversation history regardless of length — context window limits do not apply to multi-turn conversations

Maintain conversation history with intelligent summarization when needed: keep recent turns verbatim, summarize older turns into a compressed context block, and track key resolved intents in a structured memory store
Restart a fresh conversation context every 5 turns to avoid context window issues — users understand that chatbots have short memories
explain: The Converse API is stateless — the application passes the full message history with each call. As conversations grow, history eventually exceeds the context window. Intelligent management keeps the most recent turns verbatim (where contextual detail matters most), summarizes earlier turns into a compressed block, and optionally stores structured facts in a separate memory store retrieved via RAG. This preserves coherence without exceeding token limits.

q: A developer building on Amazon Bedrock serves two use cases from the same model: a medical coding assistant that must return ICD codes precisely, and a product naming brainstorming tool. What temperature settings are appropriate for each?
options: Use the same temperature for both — foundational models are insensitive to temperature differences for specialized tasks
No clear relationship exists between temperature and output quality for specialized use cases
Medical coding assistant: temperature=0 for deterministic, reproducible ICD code selection; product naming tool: temperature=0.8-1.0 for diverse, creative name suggestions
Medical coding assistant: temperature=1.5 to explore all possible codes; product naming tool: temperature=0 to generate the single best name
explain: Temperature must match the task's need for consistency vs. diversity. Medical coding requires exact, reproducible ICD code selection — temperature=0 ensures the model always selects the highest-probability (most certain) code, reducing errors. Product naming brainstorming benefits from exploring diverse, unexpected vocabulary — temperature=0.8-1.0 broadens the sampling distribution to surface creative options. Using high temperature for medical coding risks selecting less probable (potentially incorrect) codes; using low temperature for brainstorming produces repetitive suggestions.

q: A prompt engineer is tuning generation parameters for an Amazon Bedrock application. How do the temperature and top-p (nucleus sampling) parameters interact to control output randomness?
options: Temperature and top-p are mutually exclusive — only one can be set per request and the other is automatically disabled
No clear interaction exists — both parameters are processed independently and their effects do not compound
Temperature and top-p are always kept at the same value by Amazon Bedrock for consistency

Temperature scales the token probability distribution (higher = more uniform, lower = more peaked); top-p then truncates the vocabulary to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches the threshold — together they provide complementary control over output diversity
explain: Temperature and top-p are complementary parameters applied sequentially. Temperature first rescales the logits — high temperature flattens the distribution (more random), low temperature sharpens it (more deterministic). Top-p nucleus sampling then restricts token selection to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability meets the threshold (e.g., top-p=0.9 uses tokens covering 90% of probability mass). For most production use cases, adjust one parameter while keeping the other at its default, as tuning both simultaneously makes behavior harder to predict.

q: A QA engineer notices that the same prompt sent to Amazon Bedrock returns different answers on repeated calls. The application requires consistent, reproducible outputs for regression testing. Which configuration change addresses this?
options: Temperature settings have no impact on output consistency — variability is caused by network conditions
No solution exists for achieving reproducible outputs from generative AI models
Set temperature=0 to make token selection deterministic — the model always selects the highest-probability token at each step, producing the same output for identical inputs
Always use the same value for both temperature and top-p regardless of the target value
explain: Temperature=0 is the standard approach for achieving reproducible outputs from foundation models. At this setting, the model greedily selects the maximum-probability token at each decoding step, making the output deterministic given the same input and model version. This is essential for regression testing, benchmarking, and any application where the same question must always receive the same answer. Note that model version updates can still change outputs even at temperature=0, so version pinning is also important for strict reproducibility.

q: A team is deploying multiple generative AI features on Amazon Bedrock: a document classifier, a customer email writer, and a SQL query generator. Why is temperature calibration important for each feature?
options: Temperature calibration is not important — a single default temperature value works equally well for all generative AI tasks
Always use the same value — task-specific calibration introduces unnecessary complexity
Temperature calibration only matters for image generation models, not text-based foundation models

Each task has different quality criteria: the classifier needs deterministic category outputs (temperature near 0); the email writer needs friendly, varied phrasing (temperature 0.4-0.7); the SQL generator needs syntactically correct, deterministic queries (temperature near 0). Wrong calibration causes errors or poor UX
explain: Temperature calibration is task-specific because each task optimizes for different qualities. Document classification and SQL generation require correctness and consistency — high temperature introduces errors by promoting less probable (wrong) tokens. Email writing benefits from moderate temperature to produce natural, varied phrasing rather than robotic repetition. Miscalibration causes real harm: high temperature on SQL generation produces syntactically invalid queries; zero temperature on creative tasks produces monotonous, repetitive content that fails user expectations.

q: A developer asks: 'How does temperature create a trade-off between coherence and creativity in Amazon Bedrock outputs?' Which explanation is most accurate?
options: No trade-off exists — higher temperature always improves both coherence and creativity simultaneously
Temperature only affects coherence, not creativity — creativity is controlled by the model's training data

Lower temperature increases coherence by favoring high-probability, contextually expected tokens but reduces creative diversity; higher temperature increases creative diversity by exploring less probable tokens but risks grammatical errors, factual mistakes, and incoherent sentences
Always the same trade-off regardless of model — all foundation models respond identically to temperature changes
explain: The temperature parameter creates a fundamental trade-off because the highest-probability tokens are the most contextually predictable (coherent) while lower-probability tokens are more surprising (creative). At low temperature, the model produces fluent, grammatically correct, and factually consistent output by favoring expected continuations. At high temperature, the model explores surprising word choices and narrative directions — valuable for brainstorming — but also increases the probability of incoherent sentences, factual errors, and grammatical mistakes.

q: A data scientist is designing a RAG system using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases. How do text embeddings capture semantic meaning, and why is this critical for RAG retrieval quality?
options: Embeddings capture semantic meaning by converting text into high-dimensional dense vectors where semantically similar texts are positioned close together in vector space, enabling cosine similarity search to find relevant passages even when exact keywords do not match
Embeddings capture only keyword frequencies — they are a compressed representation of TF-IDF scores, not semantic meaning
Embeddings always represent the same text identically regardless of context — they are static lookup tables
Embeddings have no relationship to semantic meaning — they are random projections used only to compress text for storage efficiency
explain: Text embeddings (produced by models like Amazon Titan Embeddings or Cohere Embed) encode semantic meaning as dense floating-point vectors. Semantically similar texts — even with different words — are positioned close together in the vector space. This enables RAG to retrieve a chunk about 'myocardial infarction' when a user queries 'heart attack' — a query that would fail with keyword search. The quality of embeddings directly determines RAG retrieval recall, making embedding model selection critical for RAG performance.

q: A team is choosing between embedding models with different output dimensions (512D vs 1536D) for their Amazon Bedrock RAG application. How does embedding dimensionality affect retrieval performance?
options: All embedding models produce exactly the same retrieval quality regardless of dimensionality — only the storage cost differs
Higher embedding dimensions always produce better retrieval quality regardless of the model architecture or training data

Higher-dimensional embeddings can capture more nuanced semantic relationships and generally improve retrieval quality, but with diminishing returns and higher storage and compute costs. The optimal dimensionality should be validated empirically on the target dataset
Lower-dimensional embeddings always outperform higher-dimensional ones because they reduce noise in the vector space
explain: Embedding dimensionality affects the richness of semantic representation. Higher-dimensional embeddings (1536D vs 512D) can encode more nuanced semantic distinctions, often improving recall on complex retrieval tasks. However, the relationship is not linear — diminishing returns set in, and higher dimensions increase storage requirements and nearest-neighbor search latency. More importantly, the quality of the training data and model architecture matters more than dimension count alone. The correct approach is to benchmark both models on representative domain queries.

q: When implementing RAG with Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases, how should embeddings be generated for both documents and queries to ensure effective retrieval?
options: Documents and queries can use different embedding models — mixing models from different providers maximizes semantic coverage
Only documents need to be embedded; queries are matched using keyword search for efficiency
No relationship exists between document and query embedding consistency — any combination works

Documents and queries must be embedded using the same model with identical preprocessing — the vector space must be shared for cosine similarity comparisons to be meaningful. Embeddings from different models exist in incompatible spaces and cannot be compared
explain: Cosine similarity comparison between a query vector and document vectors is only meaningful if both were produced by the same embedding model. Different embedding models create different vector spaces where the directions and distances have different meanings — a query embedded with Titan Embeddings cannot be compared against documents embedded with Cohere Embed. Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases handles this automatically by using a single configured embedding model for all indexing and retrieval. When building custom RAG pipelines, ensuring model consistency between indexing and query time is critical.

q: A developer is implementing a vector similarity search for a RAG application using Amazon OpenSearch Service. Which distance metrics are used to measure similarity between embeddings?
options: Only Euclidean distance is used — other distance metrics are not supported in vector databases
No standard distance metrics exist — each vector database uses proprietary algorithms incompatible with each other

Cosine similarity (measures the angle between vectors), dot product (equivalent to cosine when vectors are normalized), and Euclidean distance (L2 norm) — cosine similarity is most common for text embeddings because it is invariant to vector magnitude
Only keyword frequency metrics are valid for text retrieval — geometric distance metrics are only suitable for image embeddings
explain: Vector similarity search uses several distance metrics. Cosine similarity is the most widely used for text embeddings — it measures the angle between vectors and is invariant to the magnitude (length normalization), making it robust to documents of varying lengths. Dot product is equivalent to cosine similarity when vectors are L2-normalized. Euclidean distance measures the geometric distance between point positions and is sensitive to magnitude, making it less suitable for unnormalized text embeddings. Amazon OpenSearch Service, Bedrock Knowledge Bases, and most vector databases support all three metrics.

q: A company in a specialized scientific domain uses technical jargon and acronyms not present in common training data. How do embedding models handle out-of-vocabulary terms?
options: Out-of-vocabulary terms cause embedding models to fail with an error — all terms must exist in the training vocabulary

Modern embedding models using subword tokenization (BPE, WordPiece) decompose unknown terms into known subword units and produce embeddings based on those components. Domain-specific fine-tuning of the embedding model improves representation of specialized terminology
All terms including completely novel ones always produce identical embeddings of equal quality regardless of training data
Embedding models skip out-of-vocabulary tokens and produce zero vectors, which are then excluded from similarity calculations
explain: Modern embedding models use subword tokenization (BPE or WordPiece), which decomposes unknown words into known subword components. A technical term like 'nephrolithiasis' might tokenize into familiar medical subwords that produce a reasonable embedding even if the full word is rare in training. However, truly novel acronyms or compound terms may produce poor-quality embeddings if their subwords carry different meanings. For specialized domains with extensive jargon, fine-tuning an embedding model on domain-specific text significantly improves retrieval quality.

q: A developer is confused about the difference between 'instruction following' and 'in-context learning' in prompting. Which explanation correctly distinguishes these two concepts?
options: They are synonymous — both terms describe the same prompting technique

Instruction following means the model executes explicit directives in the prompt ('Summarize this in 3 bullet points'); in-context learning means the model infers the task from examples provided in the prompt without explicit instructions — both leverage the model's ability to adapt at inference time without weight updates
Instruction following requires fine-tuning; in-context learning uses the base model without any modification
In-context learning is deprecated in modern models — only instruction following is supported by current foundation models
explain: These are related but distinct prompting paradigms. Instruction following relies on explicit task directives: 'Translate the following text to French:' — the model follows the instruction verbatim. In-context learning (few-shot) provides examples of input-output pairs and lets the model infer the pattern: showing 3 translation examples allows the model to translate a fourth without an explicit instruction. Both techniques operate purely at inference time without modifying model weights, which distinguishes them from fine-tuning.

q: A developer wants to use role specification in system prompts for Amazon Bedrock to guide model behavior for specialized tasks. How should role specification be applied effectively?
options: Role specification has no measurable effect on model output quality — it is purely cosmetic
Role specification should never be used — it causes models to generate false expertise claims

Assign a specific expert role in the system prompt ('You are a senior AWS solutions architect specializing in serverless architecture') to prime the model to apply domain-specific reasoning, use appropriate terminology, and adopt the perspective relevant to the task
Role specification only works if the model was explicitly fine-tuned on that role's domain
explain: Role specification primes the model's attention toward domain-relevant patterns from its training. 'You are an experienced tax attorney specializing in corporate law' causes the model to weight legal terminology, cautious language, and structured argumentation more heavily than it would for a generic assistant role. This improves output quality for specialized domains without fine-tuning. Effective role specifications are specific (job title plus specialization), include relevant constraints, and are placed in the system prompt for persistent effect throughout the conversation.

q: A team is designing prompts for several different generation tasks on Amazon Bedrock: code generation, summarization, and creative writing. Which prompt structures work best for each?
options: The same generic prompt structure works equally well for all generation tasks — task-specific prompt design is unnecessary
No clear guidelines exist for prompt structure across different generation tasks

Code generation: specify language, provide function signature and requirements, include example usage. Summarization: specify output length and format, state the target audience. Creative writing: set genre, tone, constraints, and provide style examples — each task benefits from structure tailored to its output requirements
All tasks should use chain-of-thought prompting regardless of complexity or output type
explain: Prompt structure should match the output requirements of each task type. Code generation benefits from explicit language specification, function signatures, and expected behavior examples — ambiguity produces incorrect syntax or wrong algorithms. Summarization prompts should define desired output length, key information to preserve, and the audience expertise level. Creative writing prompts benefit from genre, tone, and narrative constraints that focus creativity without over-constraining it. Chain-of-thought adds useful reasoning steps for complex tasks but unnecessary latency for straightforward generation.

q: A developer wants to use system prompts on Amazon Bedrock to enforce specific output constraints such as JSON-only responses and maximum response length. How should system prompts be designed for this?
options: System prompts cannot enforce output constraints — only post-processing can filter model outputs to the required format
Output constraints should always be placed in the user message, never the system prompt

Place explicit, specific output constraints in the system prompt: specify the exact format, define the schema, provide a correctly formatted example, and explicitly state what not to include. Combine with max_tokens to enforce length limits
No clear method exists for enforcing output constraints through prompting alone
explain: System prompts are the most effective location for persistent output constraints because they establish the baseline behavior for all user interactions. Effective constraint prompts are specific ('respond with valid JSON only' is better than 'use structured output'), include the exact schema or format template, provide a concrete correctly-formatted example, and explicitly enumerate prohibited elements ('no markdown, no prose explanations, no trailing text'). The max_tokens parameter enforces hard length limits. Combining system prompt instructions with few-shot examples achieves the highest constraint adherence.

q: A prompt engineer identifies several failure patterns in their Amazon Bedrock application. What are the most common pitfalls when designing prompts for reliable model behavior?
options: Ambiguous instructions that allow multiple valid interpretations, conflicting constraints in the same prompt, insufficient examples for complex output formats, overly long prompts that bury critical instructions, and failing to test prompts against adversarial or edge-case inputs
Using too many examples in the prompt — few-shot examples always degrade reliability
Specifying output format — explicit format instructions always confuse foundation models
No pitfalls exist when using large foundation models — larger models are robust to any prompt design
explain: Common prompt engineering pitfalls include: ambiguous instructions ('write a good response' vs. 'write a 3-sentence professional response addressing the customer's specific complaint'); conflicting constraints ('be concise' paired with 'include all relevant details'); critical instructions buried in long prompts where model attention is diluted; insufficient examples for complex output schemas; and untested edge cases. Regular prompt red-teaming — deliberately trying to break the prompt — surfaces these failures before they reach production users.

q: A team building a RAG system on Amazon Bedrock notices that retrieval quality is poor — relevant documents are not being returned for many queries. How should the retriever component be optimized?
options: Optimize retrieval through: upgrading to a higher-quality semantic embedding model, implementing query expansion, tuning chunk size to match query granularity, adding a re-ranking step using a cross-encoder, and evaluating retrieval quality with recall@k metrics against a golden dataset
Retrieval quality cannot be improved — it is fixed by the embedding model chosen at indexing time
Only increasing the number of retrieved documents (top-k) improves retrieval quality
Always switch to keyword-based BM25 search — vector search always produces lower quality than exact keyword matching
explain: RAG retrieval quality is improved through multiple complementary techniques. Higher-quality embedding models capture deeper semantic relationships. Query expansion generates 2-3 semantic variations of the user query, increasing recall. Chunk size tuning aligns retrieval granularity with query specificity. Re-ranking with a cross-encoder scores retrieved candidates for relevance, filtering noise before passing to the generator. All changes should be validated with recall@k metrics against a golden question dataset.

q: A RAG system's retrieval quality degrades when chunk sizes are changed. What is the relationship between document chunk size and retrieval quality?
options: Larger chunks always produce better retrieval quality — more context per chunk means more information for the model
Chunk size has no effect on retrieval quality — any size produces equivalent results

Chunk size must be optimized for the query type: smaller chunks (128-256 tokens) improve precision for specific factual queries by isolating relevant sentences; larger chunks (512-1024 tokens) improve recall for conceptual questions by keeping related information together. The optimal size depends on document structure and query distribution
Always use the maximum possible chunk size to ensure the most complete information is retrieved
explain: Chunk size creates a precision-recall trade-off in RAG. Small chunks (128-256 tokens) improve retrieval precision — each chunk covers one specific topic, so retrieved chunks are tightly relevant. However, if the answer spans multiple paragraphs, small chunks may return only part of the needed context. Large chunks (512-1024 tokens) improve recall by keeping more related information together but dilute the embedding with off-topic content, reducing precision. Hierarchical chunking (small chunks for retrieval, larger parent chunks for context) is an advanced strategy that balances both objectives.

q: A RAG application needs to answer complex questions requiring synthesizing information from multiple documents through multi-hop reasoning. How should this be handled?
options: Multi-hop questions cannot be handled by RAG systems — only single-document lookup is feasible
Always retrieve all documents in the knowledge base for multi-hop questions to ensure all relevant information is available
Use a single high top-k retrieval — retrieving 20+ documents gives the model enough context to reason across documents without special architecture

Implement multi-hop retrieval: first retrieve and partially answer the question to identify intermediate entities, then formulate a second retrieval query using those entities, and iterate. Bedrock Agents can orchestrate this multi-step retrieval loop
explain: Multi-hop questions require sequential retrieval steps because the answer to the first sub-question reveals what to search for in the second step. An agent-based approach (Bedrock Agents) can orchestrate this: the first retrieval identifies an intermediate entity; the second retrieval queries documents filtered to that entity. This iterative decomposition handles arbitrary reasoning chains. High top-k retrieval with all documents floods the context with irrelevant content, degrading generation quality. Standard single-pass RAG cannot resolve entity dependencies across documents.

q: A team is designing a RAG knowledge base for a company with documents spanning multiple departments, time periods, and access levels. What metadata should be preserved alongside embeddings for effective retrieval?
options: No metadata is needed — vector similarity alone is sufficient for all retrieval scenarios
Only document title is needed as metadata — other fields add storage overhead without retrieval benefit

Preserve metadata including: source document ID, title, creation and update date, author, department, access level and permissions, document type, and chunk position within the document — this enables metadata filtering and permission-aware retrieval
Use the same fixed metadata schema for all document types regardless of content — consistent schema is more important than relevant metadata
explain: Metadata preserved alongside embeddings enables powerful hybrid retrieval strategies beyond pure semantic search. Department metadata allows filtering results to relevant business units. Date metadata enables time-bounded queries for recent policies. Access level metadata enforces document permissions (users only receive answers from documents they are authorized to access). Chunk position metadata supports parent-child chunking strategies. Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases supports metadata filtering as a first-class feature, making metadata schema design a critical architecture decision.

q: A RAG system retrieves 20 candidate chunks per query but passes all of them to the LLM, resulting in high token costs and context noise. How should retrieval results be ranked and filtered before feeding to the model?
options: No filtering should be applied — the LLM can inherently identify and use only relevant chunks from a large context
Always pass the top-1 most similar chunk only — additional chunks always add noise and reduce answer quality

Apply a re-ranking step using a cross-encoder model to score all 20 candidates for relevance to the specific query; filter out candidates below a relevance threshold; pass only the top 3-5 re-ranked chunks to the LLM — this reduces token costs and removes noisy context that degrades generation quality
Randomly sample 5 chunks from the 20 candidates to reduce context size while maintaining diversity
explain: A two-stage retrieve-then-re-rank pipeline significantly improves RAG quality. The first stage (vector search) retrieves a broad candidate set with high recall but imperfect precision. The second stage (cross-encoder re-ranking) scores each candidate against the specific query using a model that jointly encodes query and passage — producing more accurate relevance scores than the embedding model alone. Top 3-5 re-ranked chunks reduce token costs by 75% compared to passing all 20 chunks and remove low-relevance context that can confuse the generator.

q: A team is fine-tuning a foundation model on Amazon Bedrock and needs to determine when to stop training to achieve the best balance between task performance and avoiding overfitting. What determines the appropriate training duration?
options: Training should always run for exactly 3 epochs regardless of dataset size or task complexity
Training should continue until training loss reaches zero — any remaining loss indicates undertrained performance
Always train for the maximum number of epochs allowed — more training always improves model quality

Monitor both training loss and validation loss on a held-out validation set. Stop training (early stopping) when validation loss begins to increase while training loss continues to decrease — this indicates the model has begun memorizing training examples rather than learning generalizable patterns
explain: The optimal fine-tuning duration is determined empirically by monitoring validation loss. When the model generalizes well, both training and validation losses decrease together. When the model begins overfitting, training loss continues decreasing while validation loss plateaus or increases — this divergence signals memorization rather than learning. Early stopping at this inflection point preserves the best-generalization checkpoint. Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning supports configuring validation data for this purpose. Fixed epoch counts ignore dataset size and task complexity variation.

q: A team fine-tunes a foundation model on domain-specific data and observes that the model's general language capabilities degrade significantly after fine-tuning. What is this phenomenon and how should it be prevented?
options: This is called model compression — fine-tuning always reduces model size and capability proportionally

Catastrophic forgetting: fine-tuning on narrow domain data overwrites the model's general knowledge encoded in pre-trained weights. Prevent it by using parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) to update only adapter layers while freezing base weights, or by mixing general-purpose examples into the fine-tuning dataset alongside domain examples
This phenomenon is expected and acceptable — domain specialization always requires sacrificing general capabilities
This indicates insufficient training data — adding more domain examples resolves both domain performance and general capability preservation
explain: Catastrophic forgetting occurs when gradient updates during fine-tuning overwrite the distributed representations learned during pre-training. The most effective mitigation is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation): trainable low-rank matrices are added to attention layers while the base model weights remain frozen, preserving general knowledge while adapting to the domain. Alternatively, data mixing — including 10-20% general-purpose instruction examples in the fine-tuning batch — provides a rehearsal signal that maintains general capabilities. Both techniques are supported or compatible with Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning workflows.

q: A data scientist is preparing to fine-tune a model on Amazon Bedrock with 10,000 labeled examples. How should the data be split between training and evaluation sets?
options: Use all 10,000 examples for training — evaluation data wastes examples that could improve model performance
Use 50% training, 50% evaluation — equal splits maximize evaluation reliability

Reserve a held-out evaluation set (typically 10-20% of data, e.g., 1,000-2,000 examples) for measuring generalization after fine-tuning. The evaluation set must not be used during training. For small datasets, k-fold cross-validation improves evaluation reliability
The split ratio has no effect on fine-tuning outcomes — use whichever split is most convenient
explain: A held-out evaluation set that is never seen during training is essential for measuring true generalization. Without it, only training loss is available, which underestimates overfitting. The 80/20 train/eval split is standard for 10,000 examples. The evaluation set must be stratified (covering all output classes) and representative of production data distribution. For datasets under 1,000 examples, k-fold cross-validation provides more reliable estimates. Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning accepts separate training and validation files.

q: A team completes fine-tuning a foundation model on Amazon Bedrock. What are the key indicators that fine-tuning has been successful on their specific task?
options: Training loss reaching exactly zero is the primary success indicator
Model file size increasing after fine-tuning confirms successful weight updates
The number of training steps completed without errors is the main success metric

Improved performance on task-specific evaluation metrics (accuracy, F1, BLEU, Pass@k) on a held-out test set, without significant degradation on general capability benchmarks, and validated by human evaluation on a sample of real production queries
explain: Fine-tuning success is measured by task-specific improvement on held-out test data — data the model never saw during training. Relevant metrics depend on the task: accuracy and F1 for classification, BLEU/ROUGE for summarization, Pass@k for code generation, human preference ratings for open-ended generation. Success also requires that general capabilities are preserved (no catastrophic forgetting). Human evaluation on real production queries is the gold standard because automated metrics may not capture all quality dimensions that matter in practice.

q: A team has successfully fine-tuned a model on Amazon Bedrock and wants to deploy it efficiently in production. How should fine-tuned models be deployed and updated?
options: Fine-tuned models cannot be updated — a completely new fine-tuning job must be started from the base model for each update

Deploy fine-tuned models using Amazon Bedrock provisioned throughput; for updates, use canary deployment — route a small percentage of traffic to the new model version, monitor quality and error metrics, and gradually increase traffic if stable. Maintain the previous version active for instant rollback
Deploy fine-tuned models only on Amazon SageMaker endpoints — Bedrock does not support serving fine-tuned model versions
Always retrain from the base model for each update — incremental fine-tuning on top of a previously fine-tuned model always causes instability
explain: Amazon Bedrock supports deploying fine-tuned models via provisioned throughput, which provides dedicated compute capacity and consistent latency for production traffic. Canary deployment (routing 5-10% of production traffic to the new model version) validates real-world performance before full rollout, catching regressions that held-out test sets miss. Maintaining the previous model version's provisioned throughput endpoint enables instant rollback by redirecting traffic without waiting for a new deployment. Incremental fine-tuning from a previously fine-tuned checkpoint is valid and efficient for minor updates.

q: A quality reviewer is checking outputs from an Amazon Bedrock application for signs of hallucination. What warning signs indicate a model is likely hallucinating?
options: Hallucinations always produce grammatically incorrect or obviously nonsensical text that is easy to identify

Specific claims about events, statistics, people, or citations that cannot be verified in the provided source documents; overconfident assertions without hedging language; highly specific numerical data in domains where the model should be uncertain; and responses that contradict information explicitly stated in the prompt
Hallucinations only occur in factual domains — creative writing tasks are immune to hallucination
Hallucinations can only be detected by comparing model outputs against complete training data — no output-level signals exist
explain: Hallucinations are particularly deceptive because they are fluent and confident — not obviously wrong. Warning signs include: specific but unverifiable claims (fabricated case citations, made-up statistics); overconfident assertions about uncertain facts; responses that contradict source documents provided in the prompt (intrinsic hallucinations); implausibly precise numerical details; and internal inconsistencies within a single response. Detection requires cross-referencing claims against authoritative sources and using faithfulness evaluation metrics.

q: A team wants to evaluate factual consistency of generated content from their Amazon Bedrock application at scale. How can this be automated?
options: Factual consistency cannot be evaluated automatically — only human domain experts can assess whether generated content is factually accurate
Count the number of sentences in each response — longer responses are statistically more likely to be factually consistent
Use keyword matching against source documents — any response containing the same keywords as the source is factually consistent

Use automated faithfulness evaluation: pass the generated response and source documents to a judge LLM instructed to identify claims not supported by the source; compute BERTScore or FactScore to measure semantic alignment; flag low-scoring responses for human review — combining automated signals with periodic human audits provides scalable factual consistency monitoring
explain: Automated factual consistency evaluation uses a combination of techniques. LLM-as-judge faithfulness evaluation is the most powerful: a judge model reads both the source documents and the generated response, then identifies specific claims not supported by the source. FactScore decomposes responses into atomic claims and verifies each claim against a knowledge source. BERTScore measures semantic similarity between generated content and reference documents. These automated signals scale to thousands of daily responses, with low-scoring outputs routed to human reviewers for validation.

q: A developer building a factual Q&A application on Amazon Bedrock asks whether temperature settings affect hallucination likelihood. What is the relationship?
options: Temperature has no relationship to hallucination rate — hallucinations are caused exclusively by insufficient training data

Higher temperature settings increase hallucination likelihood by promoting the selection of less probable tokens, which may be factually incorrect but statistically plausible in the language model's distribution. Lower temperature reduces but does not eliminate hallucinations
Lower temperature settings increase hallucination rate because the model becomes overconfident in its highest-probability token selections
Temperature must always be set above 1.0 to minimize hallucinations by encouraging the model to explore multiple factual hypotheses
explain: Temperature increases the probability of selecting lower-ranked tokens at each generation step. Lower-probability tokens are often less factually grounded — they may be semantically plausible completions that are factually incorrect. Higher temperature therefore increases the chance that the model generates a plausible-sounding but wrong fact. Temperature=0 (greedy decoding) selects the highest-probability token at each step, reducing randomness and typically producing more factually consistent outputs. However, temperature is not the root cause of hallucination — RAG and grounding instructions are more effective mitigations.

q: A company deploys an Amazon Bedrock application for customer questions about current events and product availability. How should the model's knowledge cutoff date be communicated and managed?
options: Explicitly state the model's knowledge cutoff date in the system prompt and in responses for time-sensitive questions; implement RAG with a regularly updated knowledge base for current product and event information; instruct the model to acknowledge uncertainty about post-cutoff events rather than generating potentially outdated responses confidently
Knowledge cutoff dates are irrelevant — foundation models continuously update their knowledge during inference
Communicate knowledge cutoff dates only in legal disclaimers buried in terms of service — in-application disclosure is unnecessary
Foundation models have no knowledge cutoff dates — all information in their responses is current and accurate
explain: Foundation models have a training data cutoff date after which they have no knowledge of events, products, or changes. For customer-facing applications, this must be managed proactively: the system prompt should acknowledge the cutoff; RAG with a current knowledge base provides up-to-date product and availability information without retraining; and the model should be instructed to hedge time-sensitive claims. Without these measures, the model confidently answers questions about current events with outdated information — a serious quality and trust issue.

q: A content team uses Amazon Bedrock for both factual documentation and creative fiction writing. How should they distinguish between hallucination (a problem) and creative speculation (intentional)?
options: Hallucination means the model generates false information presented as factual truth in a context where accuracy is required; creative speculation is intentional generation of imaginative, non-factual content in a context where creativity is the goal — the distinction is about context and intent, not the content itself
No distinction exists — all non-factual content from language models is equally problematic hallucination
Creative speculation is impossible with large language models — they can only retrieve factual information from training data
Hallucination only occurs in models under 10B parameters — large foundation models do not hallucinate
explain: The same generation behavior — producing content not grounded in verifiable facts — is hallucination in a factual context and valuable creativity in a creative context. A model inventing a fictional drug interaction is hallucination when answering a patient's medical question but legitimate storytelling when writing a thriller novel. This distinction requires clear context separation: factual applications need grounding instructions, RAG, and low temperature; creative applications intentionally enable high temperature and imaginative generation. System prompts should define the factual accuracy standard for each use case.

q: A product team wants to run human evaluation to assess the quality of their Amazon Bedrock application's responses. How should human evaluation be structured for reliable, actionable results?
options: Have evaluators rate responses on a single overall quality scale of 1-10 — a single comprehensive metric captures all relevant quality dimensions

Use structured rubrics with separate rating dimensions (accuracy, helpfulness, tone, safety) scored independently; calibrate evaluators with training examples and inter-rater agreement checks; use a stratified random sample of production queries covering diverse scenarios; and include adversarial examples to identify failure modes
Ask users of the application to voluntarily rate responses — self-selected user ratings are the most representative evaluation signal
Run human evaluation once at launch and never repeat — a model that passes initial evaluation remains valid indefinitely
explain: Structured human evaluation requires careful design to produce reliable signals. Separate rating dimensions (accuracy, helpfulness, tone, safety, groundedness) allow identification of specific weaknesses rather than a single blended score. Evaluator calibration with gold-standard examples and inter-rater agreement measurement (Cohen's kappa) ensures consistency across evaluators. Stratified random sampling ensures coverage of all query types, including edge cases. Single overall ratings conflate dimensions and mask specific problems — a response can be accurate but unhelpful, requiring separate measurement.

q: A team relies exclusively on BLEU and ROUGE scores to evaluate their Amazon Bedrock summarization model. What are the key limitations of these automatic metrics?
options: BLEU and ROUGE have no limitations — they perfectly measure all aspects of text generation quality for all tasks
BLEU and ROUGE are too computationally expensive for large-scale evaluation
BLEU and ROUGE only work for code generation tasks, not text summarization

BLEU and ROUGE measure n-gram overlap with reference texts and do not capture factual accuracy, semantic correctness, coherence, tone, or user satisfaction. A summary can have high ROUGE but contain hallucinations; a factually correct summary using different vocabulary scores low
explain: BLEU and ROUGE measure the overlap of word sequences between generated and reference text. This creates systematic blind spots: a summary using different but equivalent vocabulary scores low despite being correct; a summary copying reference phrases scores high despite missing key information; factually incorrect but word-similar summaries score well; fluent hallucinations are undetectable by n-gram metrics. Supplement BLEU/ROUGE with faithfulness metrics (BERTScore, FactScore), human evaluation on quality dimensions, and task-specific metrics aligned with business objectives.

q: A team conducting human evaluation of their Amazon Bedrock application finds that different evaluators give very different scores for the same responses. How should inter-rater agreement be measured and improved?
options: Average all evaluator scores without measuring agreement — averaging removes variability automatically
Replace human evaluators entirely with automated metrics — disagreement proves human evaluation is unreliable
Inter-rater agreement cannot be measured quantitatively — it is inherently subjective

Measure inter-rater agreement using Cohen's kappa (for categorical ratings) or Pearson correlation (for continuous ratings); investigate disagreements to identify ambiguous rubric criteria; clarify rubrics with annotated examples; conduct calibration sessions where evaluators discuss disagreement cases; target kappa > 0.6 before trusting evaluation results
explain: Inter-rater disagreement in human evaluation typically signals ambiguous evaluation criteria rather than inherent subjectivity. Cohen's kappa measures agreement corrected for chance — a kappa of 0.0 means chance-level agreement, 1.0 means perfect agreement, and 0.6 or above is considered substantial. Low kappa prompts investigation: which dimension has the most disagreement? Clarifying rubrics with concrete examples of strong vs. weak responses for each criterion dramatically improves agreement. Calibration sessions where evaluators discuss disagreement cases align mental models before formal evaluation begins.

q: A team is setting up evaluation for their Amazon Bedrock application and debates whether to use intrinsic or extrinsic evaluation. What is the difference and when should each be used?
options: Intrinsic and extrinsic evaluation are synonymous terms for the same methodology

Intrinsic evaluation measures model properties directly (perplexity, BLEU, human quality ratings on held-out examples) independent of a downstream task; extrinsic evaluation measures impact on real business metrics (conversion rate, task completion, customer satisfaction) — intrinsic is faster and cheaper; extrinsic is more meaningful but harder to attribute
Intrinsic evaluation is always superior — business metrics are too noisy to use for AI evaluation
Extrinsic evaluation is only for deployed models; intrinsic evaluation is only for models still in training
explain: Intrinsic evaluation measures model quality in isolation using standardized metrics (perplexity on test sets, ROUGE for summarization, human ratings of response quality) that are fast and reproducible — valuable for quick model comparison during development. Extrinsic evaluation measures impact on downstream business outcomes (does better summarization lead to higher user engagement? does improved code generation reduce developer time?) — harder to measure and slower to collect but directly validate business value. The best frameworks use both: intrinsic metrics for rapid iteration, extrinsic metrics for final deployment decisions.

q: A team uses Amazon Bedrock for customer support automation and notices a 15% error rate on their evaluation set. How should systematic error analysis be conducted to understand and fix the failures?
options: Simply retrain with more data — error analysis is unnecessary because more training data resolves all failure modes

Categorize all errors by failure type (hallucination, wrong format, off-topic response, incorrect reasoning); analyze which input characteristics correlate with failures; prioritize failure categories by frequency and business impact; for each high-priority category, identify the root cause and implement targeted fixes
Error analysis is only needed when error rate exceeds 50% — a 15% error rate is within acceptable bounds for AI systems
Random sampling of errors is sufficient — systematic categorization introduces bias into the analysis
explain: Systematic error analysis follows a structured process: categorize errors into meaningful failure types (hallucinations, format violations, incorrect reasoning, off-topic deflection); measure the frequency and business impact of each category; and identify root causes for high-priority categories. Does hallucination correlate with specific topics missing from the knowledge base? Does incorrect format correlate with ambiguous prompt instructions? This informs targeted interventions: RAG knowledge base expansion, prompt clarification, or fine-tuning data collection for underperforming scenarios.

q: A company's finance team asks how Amazon Bedrock pricing works to forecast AI costs. What is the pricing model for Amazon Bedrock?
options: Amazon Bedrock charges a fixed monthly subscription regardless of usage volume
Amazon Bedrock charges only for model training — inference is free for all supported models
Amazon Bedrock pricing is based on the number of API calls, not token volume

Amazon Bedrock charges per input token and per output token consumed during model inference. Different models have different per-token rates. Provisioned throughput (reserved capacity) offers lower per-token rates for high-volume predictable workloads compared to on-demand pricing
explain: Amazon Bedrock uses a consumption-based model priced per 1,000 input tokens and per 1,000 output tokens. Model selection dramatically affects cost: Claude 3 Haiku costs approximately $0.25 per million input tokens vs Claude 3 Opus at $15 per million input tokens — a 60x difference. Output tokens are typically priced higher than input tokens because generation is more compute-intensive than prompt processing. Provisioned throughput requires committing to a minimum throughput level — cost-effective for consistent high-volume workloads. Fine-tuning jobs are priced separately per training token.

q: Amazon Bedrock provides access to models from multiple providers including Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, Amazon, Cohere, and AI21 Labs. How should teams select the right model for their specific use case?
options: Always select the largest model available — parameter count is the only reliable predictor of production quality
Select models based on provider brand recognition alone — established brands always produce better models

Match model characteristics to use case requirements: Claude models for complex reasoning and long-context analysis; Titan Embeddings for embedding generation; Mistral for cost-effective text generation; Cohere for enterprise search. Validate selection empirically on representative task samples using task-specific metrics before production commitment
All Bedrock models produce identical outputs for the same prompt — model selection has no practical impact on quality
explain: Amazon Bedrock model selection requires matching model strengths to task requirements. Claude models (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus) excel at complex reasoning, nuanced instruction following, and long-context analysis. Amazon Titan Embeddings is optimized for embedding generation and semantic search. Mistral models offer cost-effective text generation for simpler tasks. Cohere excels at enterprise retrieval and reranking. However, no mapping is absolute — empirical validation on representative task examples with task-specific metrics is essential before production commitment.

q: A solutions architect is designing a production application using Amazon Bedrock. How should Bedrock be integrated with other AWS services to build a complete generative AI solution?
options: Amazon Bedrock operates as an isolated service that cannot integrate with other AWS services

Bedrock integrates natively with AWS Lambda (serverless invocation triggers), S3 (knowledge base document storage and fine-tuning data), Amazon OpenSearch (vector store for RAG), IAM (access control), CloudWatch (logging and metrics), and Bedrock Agents (orchestration) — these integrations enable complete end-to-end generative AI architectures
Bedrock can only be accessed directly through the AWS Console and does not expose APIs for programmatic integration
Bedrock requires Amazon SageMaker for all integrations — direct integration with other AWS services is not supported
explain: Amazon Bedrock integrates natively across the AWS ecosystem. Lambda functions invoke Bedrock APIs in response to events (API Gateway, S3 uploads, SQS messages). S3 stores fine-tuning datasets and knowledge base documents. Amazon OpenSearch Service or Aurora pgvector serve as vector stores for Knowledge Bases. IAM controls model access at the identity level. CloudWatch logs metrics and invocation data. EventBridge triggers knowledge base synchronization when new documents arrive in S3. Bedrock Agents orchestrate multi-step workflows calling Lambda action groups.

q: What monitoring and optimization features does Amazon Bedrock provide for production generative AI applications?
options: Amazon Bedrock provides no monitoring features — all monitoring must be implemented with third-party tools
Bedrock only provides basic API availability monitoring via the AWS status page

Bedrock provides: model invocation logging (prompt and response capture to S3 via CloudWatch), CloudWatch metrics for latency, token counts, and error rates, AWS CloudTrail for API audit logs, model evaluation tools for quality assessment, and Bedrock Guardrails metrics for content policy enforcement
Bedrock monitoring is only available for provisioned throughput deployments, not on-demand API usage
explain: Amazon Bedrock provides a comprehensive monitoring stack. Model invocation logging captures full prompt and response data to S3 for offline analysis. CloudWatch metrics expose latency distributions, token consumption, throttling events, and error rates — enabling dashboards and alarms. CloudTrail logs every API call for security audit and compliance. Bedrock Model Evaluation provides automated and human evaluation workflows for quality assessment. Guardrails emit metrics on content policy violations and PII redactions. Together these support both operational and quality monitoring.

q: A production application on Amazon Bedrock depends on specific behavior from a particular foundation model version. How does Bedrock handle model updates and version management?
options: Amazon Bedrock automatically updates all models to the latest version — applications always use the newest model with no configuration required

Bedrock supports model version pinning via model IDs that include version specifiers — applications should reference specific versioned model IDs rather than alias endpoints to ensure reproducible behavior. When AWS deprecates a model version, advance notice is provided with a migration path to the replacement version
All Amazon Bedrock models are permanently frozen — no model updates ever occur after initial release
Version management is not supported — all applications must use the same single current model version for each model family
explain: Amazon Bedrock model IDs include version information. Applications requiring reproducible behavior should reference the full versioned model ID rather than a generic alias. When providers update their models, the previous version remains available under its versioned ID for a deprecation period with advance notice. Recommended practice: pin model IDs in configuration (not hardcoded); maintain a model abstraction layer; test against the new model version before the deprecation deadline; document model-specific behaviors as they may differ between versions.

q: A company is deploying Amazon Q Business for 2,000 employees and needs to optimize their knowledge base structure. How should knowledge bases be organized for optimal Amazon Q Business performance?
options: Put all documents in a single flat index with no organization — Amazon Q Business handles all structure internally

Organize knowledge bases by topic domain with consistent document formatting, clear headings and structure, regular content freshness maintenance, and accurate metadata tagging. Use separate indexes for fundamentally different content types to improve retrieval precision
Knowledge base organization has no effect on Amazon Q Business answer quality
Structure all knowledge base documents as bullet points only — narrative prose degrades Q Business retrieval accuracy
explain: Amazon Q Business retrieval quality is directly affected by knowledge base organization. Domain-separated indexes (HR policies, technical docs, product information) allow more targeted retrieval. Consistent document formatting with clear headings enables better chunking. Regular content freshness maintenance (archiving outdated documents, updating changed policies) prevents retrieval of stale information. Metadata tagging (department, document type, date) enables filtered retrieval for user groups with different information access needs. Poor organization causes retrieval noise that degrades answer quality.

q: A company wants to connect multiple data sources to Amazon Q Business. What document formats and data sources does Amazon Q Business support for knowledge ingestion?
options: Amazon Q Business supports PDF documents, Microsoft Office files (Word, Excel, PowerPoint), HTML, plain text, and Markdown formats. It connects natively to enterprise systems including SharePoint, Salesforce, Confluence, ServiceNow, Jira, and S3 via built-in connectors
Amazon Q Business only supports plain text files in UTF-8 encoding — all other formats must be converted before ingestion
Amazon Q Business requires all content to be pre-converted to JSONL format before ingestion
Amazon Q Business only supports S3 as a data source — all other systems must export to S3 before connection
explain: Amazon Q Business provides broad format support to meet enterprise needs: PDF, Word, Excel, PowerPoint, HTML, plain text, and Markdown are all natively ingested. The built-in connector library includes SharePoint Online, Confluence, Salesforce, ServiceNow, Jira, Slack, and S3 — enabling connection to existing enterprise content without custom ETL pipelines. Connectors handle crawling, format conversion, chunking, and embedding automatically. This eliminates the need to export and transform content before connection.

q: A company wants Amazon Q Business to reflect their company-specific terminology, communication style, and specialized domain knowledge. How can Amazon Q Business be customized for their specific business domain?
options: Amazon Q Business customization requires replacing the underlying foundation model with a fine-tuned version — standard Q Business has no customization features

Connect authoritative company documents (policies, product documentation, playbooks, style guides) as the primary knowledge base; configure custom guardrails for domain-specific topics; and use Amazon Q Apps to create specialized interfaces for specific workflows — the model adapts to company terminology through the knowledge base content rather than model retraining
Amazon Q Business cannot be customized — all responses reflect generic AWS knowledge only
Customization is only available for Fortune 500 companies with enterprise AWS agreements
explain: Amazon Q Business achieves domain customization primarily through knowledge base content rather than model fine-tuning. When company documents (product specifications, internal policies, proprietary terminology glossaries) are connected as data sources, Q Business retrieves and synthesizes from this authoritative content — naturally adopting the company's vocabulary. Guardrails can restrict responses to approved topics and block competitor mentions. Amazon Q Apps allow creating task-specific interfaces that scope Q Business to particular workflows. This approach requires no ML expertise and updates automatically as knowledge base documents change.

q: A company deploys Amazon Q Business and needs to ensure employees only receive answers from documents they are authorized to access. What access control and security features are available?
options: Amazon Q Business provides no document-level access control — all employees see the same answers from all connected documents
Access control requires building a custom middleware layer that filters Q Business responses after generation based on user permissions

Amazon Q Business enforces document-level access controls by synchronizing ACLs from connected data sources; user identity is resolved through IAM Identity Center integration; at query time, retrieval is restricted to documents the requesting user is authorized to access — ensuring users never see content from restricted documents even indirectly through AI-generated responses
Only administrators can use Amazon Q Business — it does not support end-user access
explain: Amazon Q Business enforces access controls at the retrieval layer, not just the interface layer. It crawls and stores document-level ACLs from connected systems (SharePoint groups, Salesforce record visibility, Confluence space permissions). When a user authenticates via IAM Identity Center, their identity is used to filter the candidate document pool before retrieval — the user only receives answers synthesized from documents they are authorized to access. This architecture prevents indirect information leakage where an AI answer might synthesize restricted content without explicitly showing the document.

q: A company is evaluating Amazon Q Business before a company-wide rollout to 5,000 employees. How should they assess its readiness for enterprise deployment?
options: No evaluation is needed — Amazon Q Business is a managed AWS service and is automatically enterprise-ready
Run Amazon Q Business in production immediately with all employees — only real usage reveals enterprise readiness
Evaluate it using only AWS-provided benchmark scores — internal evaluation is unnecessary for managed services

Conduct a structured pilot: connect the top 5 knowledge sources; run a golden question set covering key use cases with ground-truth answers; measure answer accuracy, citation quality, and access control enforcement; test with 50 pilot users across departments; measure adoption metrics and compare against the baseline search tool being replaced
explain: Enterprise evaluation of Amazon Q Business requires a structured pilot before full rollout. A golden question set with verified answers establishes objective quality baselines. Testing top knowledge sources reveals indexing gaps and retrieval quality issues. Access control enforcement testing verifies that users cannot access restricted documents through Q responses. Pilot users from different departments generate realistic query diversity that benchmark questions miss. Adoption metrics (usage vs. the tool being replaced) and satisfaction surveys validate business value before company-wide commitment.

q: A financial services company needs a foundation model for two tasks: summarizing 50-page regulatory documents and classifying short customer support tickets into 12 predefined categories. Both tasks must run on-premises due to data residency requirements. Which selection criteria should drive the model choice?
options: Select the model with the largest parameter count available, as more parameters guarantee better performance on both long-document summarization and short-text classification tasks

Evaluate models on task-specific benchmarks using a sample of internal documents; prioritize context window length for summarization and fine-tunability for classification; confirm the model license permits on-premises deployment
Select the model with the highest public benchmark score (MMLU or HellaSwag) since top benchmark performance correlates directly with production accuracy on all enterprise tasks
Choose the model that requires the least GPU VRAM, since on-premises hardware is constrained and inference efficiency is more important than accuracy for enterprise tasks
explain: Foundation model selection requires matching model capabilities to specific task requirements. For long-document summarization, context window length is critical — a model with a 4K context window cannot process a 50-page document without chunking. For classification, fine-tunability on internal examples improves accuracy over zero-shot prompting. On-premises deployment adds a licensing constraint that eliminates cloud-only models. Public benchmark scores measure general reasoning ability on academic tasks and do not reliably predict performance on specialized enterprise workloads. Parameter count is one proxy for capability but ignores architecture efficiency and task fit. GPU VRAM efficiency matters for hardware planning but should not override accuracy requirements.

q: A legal technology company wants to adapt a foundation model to answer questions about their specific contract clauses and legal precedents. Their dataset is 5,000 labeled Q&A pairs. The team is evaluating whether to use prompt engineering (in-context learning) or fine-tuning. Which factors should determine the approach?
options: Always use fine-tuning when labeled data is available, as prompt engineering is only appropriate when no training data exists and always produces lower-quality results
Fine-tune on all 5,000 examples immediately, as labeled data should always be used for gradient updates rather than wasted in prompts where context window limits prevent using more than 5-10 examples
Use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) instead of either approach, since RAG always outperforms both prompt engineering and fine-tuning for legal domain questions
