q: A company's RAG system has high retrieval recall (relevant documents are retrieved) but low answer quality — the LLM generates incorrect answers even when relevant documents are in the context. What is the MOST likely root cause and fix?
options: The LLM is not effectively using the retrieved context — improve through prompt engineering: explicitly instruct the model to 'use ONLY the following documents to answer the question' and place the retrieved documents prominently in the prompt before the question
The vector database is returning documents sorted by recency instead of relevance — re-sort results by similarity score
The embedding model is outdated — upgrade to the latest version to improve semantic matching between queries and retrieved documents
The chunking size is too large — reduce chunk size from 1,000 to 100 tokens to force the model to focus on specific sentences
explain: When retrieval is good (recall is high) but generation is poor, the problem is in the generation stage — the LLM is not effectively utilizing the retrieved context. The primary fix is prompt engineering: explicit grounding instructions ('Answer using ONLY the provided documents. If the answer is not in the documents, say so.') significantly improve how well the model uses context. Document placement also matters — putting retrieved content closer to the question (recency effect in attention) can help. Chunking adjustments affect retrieval, not generation quality when retrieval is already high. Sorting is a retrieval issue. Embedding model quality affects recall, not generation quality when recall is already high.

q: A company has labeled 5,000 customer support conversations where agents resolved issues correctly. They want to use this data to improve a foundation model's customer support responses. Which fine-tuning approach on Amazon Bedrock is MOST appropriate?
options: Few-shot prompting with 10 representative examples from the 5,000 — using more examples adds noise rather than improving quality

Supervised fine-tuning using the 5,000 conversation examples in instruction format (customer question → correct agent response) to teach the model the patterns of effective support responses for the company's specific products and policies
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) using the 5,000 examples as reward signal data, training a reward model and using PPO to optimize the foundation model
Continued pre-training on the 5,000 conversations as unlabeled text to improve the model's understanding of the support domain
explain: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on instruction-response pairs is the most direct and effective use of labeled conversation data. The model learns the company's specific product vocabulary, policies, and response style from the 5,000 examples. Amazon Bedrock supports SFT for supported models. RLHF requires a separate reward model training pipeline and human preference comparison data — it's significantly more complex and the 5,000 examples would need to be in preference pair format (preferred vs rejected). Continued pre-training on unlabeled text improves language understanding but doesn't teach response quality. 5,000 examples is sufficient for quality fine-tuning — all should be used, not just 10.

q: A company deploys an AI chatbot that answers questions about their products. During a safety review, they discover that users can ask the model to role-play as a different AI without safety guidelines, and the model complies, then answers questions it would normally refuse. What type of jailbreak is this and how should it be prevented?
options: This is normal creative use of the AI; users should be allowed to explore role-playing scenarios and the safety guidelines are too restrictive

This is a role-playing jailbreak (DAN-style attack); prevent it using Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with denied topics configured to block requests attempting to establish alternative AI personas, combined with system prompt instructions that establish the AI's persistent identity and refuse persona switches
This is a prompt overflow attack; prevent it by implementing rate limiting on API calls to reduce the frequency of jailbreak attempts
This is a context injection attack; prevent it by limiting the maximum input length so users cannot include long role-playing setup instructions
explain: Role-playing jailbreaks ask the model to pretend to be a different system without restrictions (e.g., 'DAN — Do Anything Now'). The model complies with the role-play framing and bypasses its safety training. Prevention requires multiple layers: (1) System prompt instructions establishing persistent identity and explicitly refusing persona changes. (2) Bedrock Guardrails with denied topics configured to detect and block role-play jailbreak patterns. (3) Output filtering to catch content that shouldn't be generated regardless of framing. Input length limiting doesn't prevent short jailbreaks. Rate limiting reduces frequency but doesn't prevent jailbreaks. This is not legitimate creative use — it's deliberate safety bypass.

q: A company has deployed a RAG-based question-answering system and wants to continuously monitor its quality in production. They want metrics that detect when the system starts providing incorrect or irrelevant answers without requiring human evaluation of every response. Which automated evaluation approach is MOST effective?
options: Monitor response length: responses shorter than 50 tokens indicate the model is refusing to answer and longer than 500 tokens indicate hallucination
Use Amazon CloudWatch to track API latency — increased latency correlates with the model generating longer hallucinated responses

Implement LLM-as-judge evaluation: use a separate LLM (via Amazon Bedrock) to score production responses on faithfulness (does the answer match the retrieved context?), relevance (does the answer address the question?), and groundedness — flag low-scoring responses for human review
Calculate BLEU score between production responses and a static set of reference answers — low BLEU scores indicate quality degradation
explain: LLM-as-judge is the most scalable approach for evaluating open-ended responses. A separate evaluator LLM scores responses on faithfulness (answer consistent with retrieved documents), relevance (answer addresses the question), and completeness — dimensions that automated token-matching metrics cannot assess. Low-scoring responses are flagged for human review, focusing human attention where it matters. BLEU requires reference answers for every production query (impossible at scale) and measures n-gram overlap, not semantic correctness. Response length is a poor proxy for quality — correct short answers and long detailed answers are both valid. API latency reflects compute, not content quality.

q: A data scientist is comparing two approaches for code generation: Model A (fine-tuned on company code) and Model B (Claude with few-shot prompts). They need an objective metric to compare which generates more functionally correct code. Which evaluation metric is MOST appropriate?
options: CodeBERT embedding similarity between generated code and reference implementations
Pass@k metric (the probability that at least one of k generated code samples passes all unit tests) — this directly measures functional correctness by executing the code against a test suite
Human evaluation where developers rate the readability and style of generated code on a 1-5 scale
BLEU score comparing generated code to reference code from the test set — higher BLEU indicates more similar code structure
explain: Pass@k is the gold standard for code generation evaluation. It generates k code samples for each problem, runs the unit tests against each, and calculates the probability that at least one sample passes all tests. This directly measures functional correctness — code that works, not code that looks like the reference. Pass@1 (does the first attempt work?) is most relevant for production use. BLEU measures string overlap — syntactically different but functionally equivalent code receives a low BLEU score despite being correct. Embedding similarity has the same problem. Human readability evaluation doesn't capture whether the code actually works, and is expensive at scale.

q: A developer is new to generative AI and asks: 'What is an LLM token and why does it matter for cost and performance?' Which answer is MOST accurate?
options: A token is one character — a 1,000-character document uses 1,000 tokens. Models have unlimited context and can process documents of any size

A token is roughly a word or word-fragment (4 characters on average in English) — LLMs process and generate text in tokens; cost is typically billed per input and output token, and performance (latency) increases with token count. A sentence of 100 words ≈ 133 tokens. Context window limits are also measured in tokens
Tokens are internal model representations that don't affect cost or performance — only the number of API calls determines billing
A token is one complete word — a 1,000-word document contains exactly 1,000 tokens. Cost is fixed per request regardless of length
explain: Tokens are the fundamental unit of LLM computation. Tokenizers split text into subword units averaging ~4 characters in English (word boundaries + common subwords). This means 1 word ≈ 1.3 tokens on average. Understanding tokens is critical for: (1) Cost estimation (billed per input+output token). (2) Context window management (32K, 128K, 200K token limits). (3) Latency prediction (generation speed in tokens/second). A 1,000-word document ≈ 1,333 tokens, not 1,000. Tokens are not single characters (that would be much more expensive). Billing is definitely per-token, not per-call. Context windows have finite token limits.

q: A company is deploying a generative AI system for loan application decisions. A compliance officer raises concerns about model transparency and accountability. Which practices should be implemented to ensure responsible AI deployment?
options: Deploy the system without human oversight since AI systems make unbiased decisions and regulatory compliance is ensured by the model provider (AWS)
Use a very large model (100B+ parameters) — larger models make more accurate and fair decisions that don't require explanation or auditing
Have the legal team review the model's training data once and certify it as compliant — ongoing monitoring is not required after initial certification

Implement explainability mechanisms (SHAP values or attention visualization for which input factors influenced the decision), maintain a complete audit log of all decisions, enable human review for borderline cases, document model limitations in the system design, and ensure the model is regularly tested for disparate impact across protected groups
explain: Responsible AI for high-stakes decisions (loans, employment, healthcare) requires multiple controls: (1) Explainability — why was a decision made (required by ECOA, FCRA). (2) Audit logs — complete records for regulatory review. (3) Human oversight — automated systems should flag edge cases for human review. (4) Bias testing — regular disparate impact analysis across race, gender, age, and other protected classes. (5) Model cards documenting limitations. Model size does not determine fairness. AWS provides the platform but compliance responsibility lies with the deploying company. One-time certification is insufficient — models drift, data distributions change, and regulations evolve.

q: A company is establishing an AI governance framework for their Amazon Bedrock deployments. They need to ensure all AI applications follow ethical guidelines, data privacy requirements, and regulatory constraints. Which components should be in their governance framework?
options: Implement AI governance through API rate limiting — restricting the volume of AI requests automatically limits the scale of potential harm

Establish an AI review board for approving high-risk AI deployments, define data classification policies specifying what data can be sent to foundation models, implement Amazon Bedrock Guardrails for runtime enforcement, maintain model cards documenting intended use and limitations, and conduct regular bias and safety audits
Restrict all AI applications to use only models trained exclusively on the company's proprietary data to avoid data privacy concerns
Use only open-source foundation models to ensure full transparency and control over all model weights and training data
explain: Comprehensive AI governance requires multiple components: (1) An AI review board ensures human oversight for high-risk systems. (2) Data classification policies prevent sensitive data (PII, trade secrets) from being sent to foundation models inappropriately. (3) Bedrock Guardrails provide automated runtime enforcement of content and behavior policies. (4) Model cards document purpose, limitations, and responsible use guidelines. (5) Regular audits detect bias and safety issues that may emerge over time. Open-source models improve transparency but don't solve governance. Company-only training data is impractical and most foundation models are trained on broad data that's beneficial. Rate limiting addresses volume, not ethical or privacy risks.

q: A company is choosing between Amazon Bedrock foundation models for a document translation task (English to 10 languages). They need high accuracy, especially for technical documentation. Model A supports 20 languages with average quality. Model B supports 15 languages with higher quality per language. Model C is specifically fine-tuned for technical translation with 10-language support. Which model should they prioritize evaluating?
options: Model C — specialization for technical translation directly aligns with the use case; evaluate it first on a representative sample of their technical documents using BLEU/COMET scores and human post-editing time as quality metrics
All three models are equivalent for this task — run a coin flip to select one and focus engineering effort elsewhere
Model A — maximum language coverage ensures flexibility for future expansion beyond the current 10 languages
Model B — higher per-language quality indicates better general translation capability that should transfer to technical domains
explain: Technical documentation translation has specialized challenges: domain-specific terminology, precise meaning requirements, and consistent terminology usage across a document. A model fine-tuned on technical translation is purpose-built for these challenges. Evaluate it first on a representative sample using translation quality metrics (BLEU for automated comparison, COMET for neural evaluation, human post-editing time for business impact). Model A's language breadth isn't needed if 10 languages are sufficient. Model B's general quality may not transfer to technical domains — general models often struggle with specialized terminology. All models are not equivalent — this is a testable hypothesis that should drive selection.

q: A company's CEO asks: 'Why do large language models sometimes make up facts that sound convincing but are wrong?' Which explanation is MOST technically accurate?
options: LLMs generate text by predicting the most probable next token based on training data patterns — they optimize for plausible-sounding text, not factual accuracy. When a model doesn't 'know' an answer, it generates text that fits the statistical pattern of an answer, which may be false but sounds confident
LLMs hallucinate because their training data contains errors — hallucinations are simply the model repeating incorrect information from training data
LLMs intentionally generate false information to protect proprietary training data — hallucinations are a deliberate privacy feature
Hallucinations only occur when the model is overloaded with too many requests — they are a performance issue, not a fundamental limitation
explain: Hallucination arises from the fundamental mechanism of language models: they're trained to predict the next token in a sequence to maximize likelihood over training data. This makes them excellent at generating fluent, confident-sounding text. However, 'sounds like a factual answer' and 'is factually accurate' are different optimization targets. When the model lacks the specific knowledge, it generates statistically plausible text rather than 'I don't know.' This is not intentional, not a performance issue, and not purely repetition of training errors — it's confabulation from pattern matching. Hallucinations occur even under light load and for topics with accurate training data.

q: A company uses Amazon Bedrock to build an application. They want to ensure their prompts and outputs are never used by AWS to train or improve foundation models. What should they know about Amazon Bedrock's data privacy?
options: Amazon Bedrock uses all customer data for model improvement unless the customer uses a VPC endpoint, which encrypts data and prevents AWS from accessing it
Only prompts are protected from training usage; model outputs may be used by AWS and model providers to improve models unless explicitly prohibited

Amazon Bedrock does not use customer prompt data or model outputs to train or improve foundation models by default — customer data is used only to service their requests and is protected under the AWS shared responsibility model
Customers must explicitly opt out of training data usage by contacting AWS Support and completing a Data Privacy Agreement
explain: This is an important fact for enterprise customers: Amazon Bedrock does not train or fine-tune foundation models on customer data (prompts, responses, or fine-tuning datasets). Customer data sent to Bedrock is used only to generate responses and is not shared with model providers (Anthropic, AI21, etc.) for training purposes. This is documented in AWS's data privacy policies and is a key differentiator for compliance-conscious customers. No opt-out is required because training on customer data is not performed by default. VPC endpoints provide network-level privacy but don't change Bedrock's data usage policy.

q: A company wants to allow developers to experiment with multiple foundation models (Claude, Titan, Mistral) through a single API without managing separate integrations for each provider. They also want to track costs per team and enforce usage limits. Which combination of AWS services meets these requirements?
options: Contact each model provider (Anthropic, AWS, Mistral) separately for API access, implement a custom proxy server on EC2 to unify the APIs, and use CloudTrail for cost allocation
Use Amazon Bedrock with AWS Lambda as middleware per model provider to handle different API formats, and use CloudWatch for cost tracking

Amazon Bedrock provides a unified API for all supported models — use IAM policies per team with Bedrock model access restrictions, tag all Bedrock API calls with cost allocation tags by team, use AWS Budgets to set per-team spending alerts
Deploy each model separately using Amazon SageMaker JumpStart endpoints, create API Gateway APIs for each model, and build a custom routing layer to distribute requests
explain: Amazon Bedrock is specifically designed as a unified API for multiple foundation model providers — developers use the same InvokeModel or Converse API regardless of whether they're calling Claude, Titan, or Mistral. Model access is controlled per team via IAM. Cost allocation tags on Bedrock API calls enable team-level cost tracking. AWS Budgets sends alerts when teams approach spending thresholds. This meets all requirements natively. Separate SageMaker deployments, custom routing layers, and per-provider integrations are all unnecessary complexity when Bedrock's unified API handles this.

q: A developer asks about the difference between 'training' and 'inference' in the context of foundation models. Which explanation is CORRECT?
options: Training and inference are synonymous — both refer to the process of the model generating responses using its knowledge

Training is the computationally intensive process of learning model weights from a large dataset (done once or periodically); inference is using the trained model to generate responses to new inputs (done continuously at scale). Amazon Bedrock provides managed inference — users don't manage training unless fine-tuning
Inference is the process of evaluating model performance on test data; training is the process of deploying the model to production
Training is done in real-time during inference — the model continuously updates its weights as it processes each user query
explain: Training (pre-training) involves optimizing billions of model parameters over massive datasets using thousands of GPUs over weeks/months — done by model providers like Anthropic or AWS. Inference is using those fixed weights to generate responses to new inputs — fast (seconds), done at scale. Amazon Bedrock handles inference infrastructure; users consume trained models via API. Models don't update weights during inference (that would be online learning, a different paradigm). Inference is not model evaluation — that's a separate benchmarking step. Production deployment is part of the MLOps workflow, but inference is the actual generation step.

q: A company wants to build a generative AI application that answers questions about their business using their internal data. They are debating between RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) and fine-tuning. Which scenario BEST suits RAG over fine-tuning?
options: The company has 1 million labeled examples of correct question-answer pairs that would significantly improve model accuracy through training
The company needs the model to adopt a fundamentally different response style (extremely concise, structured output) that doesn't emerge from prompting alone
The company's data changes frequently (weekly updates to product catalogs, policies, pricing) and they want the AI to always reflect the latest information without retraining
The company wants to teach the model a specialized vocabulary and notation system used internally that is not in the model's training data
explain: RAG is ideal when data changes frequently — the retrieval index is updated in near-real-time without retraining the model. Product catalogs updated weekly would require weekly fine-tuning runs if using fine-tuning, which is expensive and operationally complex. RAG simply re-indexes the new documents. Fine-tuning is better when: the model needs to learn new vocabulary/notation not in training data (Option B), adopt a fundamentally different style that prompting can't achieve (Option C), or when large amounts of labeled training data exist to improve task accuracy (Option D). Each of those scenarios justifies the training cost because the capability is embedded in model weights.

q: A company uses Amazon Bedrock with the Converse API to build a multi-turn customer service chatbot. After each conversation, they want to analyze the full conversation history to extract insights. Which Amazon Bedrock feature should they use to store and retrieve conversation context efficiently?
options: Store conversations in Amazon DynamoDB and use a Lambda trigger to automatically inject conversation history into each Bedrock API call
Use Amazon Bedrock conversation memory feature to automatically store and retrieve conversation history between sessions

Implement conversation history management in the application layer by storing the messages array and passing it with each subsequent API call to the Converse API — Bedrock's stateless API design requires the application to maintain conversation state
Enable Amazon Bedrock session management to persist conversation state across API calls without passing history each time
explain: Amazon Bedrock's Converse API is stateless — it does not store conversation history between API calls. The application is responsible for maintaining the messages array (conversation history) and including it in each request. This stateless design provides flexibility but requires application-level state management. Bedrock does not have built-in 'conversation memory' or 'session management' features that persist state between API calls. DynamoDB storage with Lambda injection could work, but the fundamental architectural point is that the application manages state and passes it to Bedrock — understanding this is key for the exam.

q: A machine learning engineer is fine-tuning a foundation model using Amazon Bedrock. They notice the fine-tuned model performs excellently on the training examples but poorly on new, unseen inputs. What is this problem called and how should it be addressed?
options: This is overfitting — the model memorized the training examples instead of generalizing; address by adding more diverse training examples, applying regularization techniques, reducing training epochs, or using validation loss to implement early stopping
This is data drift — the training examples are from a different distribution than production inputs; address by collecting production examples and adding them to the training set
This is catastrophic forgetting — fine-tuning erased the model's general knowledge; address by using LoRA instead of full fine-tuning
This is underfitting — the model didn't train long enough; address by increasing training epochs and learning rate
explain: When a model performs well on training data but poorly on unseen data, it has overfit — it memorized training examples (including their noise and idiosyncrasies) rather than learning generalizable patterns. Remedies include: (1) More diverse training data to cover the variety of real inputs. (2) Regularization (dropout, weight decay). (3) Early stopping using validation loss to stop before memorization occurs. (4) Fewer training epochs. Underfitting is the opposite problem (poor training AND test performance). Catastrophic forgetting is a different phenomenon where general capabilities degrade. Data drift describes a different distribution between training and production (which is a related but distinct issue).

q: A company is building a chatbot using Amazon Bedrock. During development, they discover that the model's responses are correct but too verbose — users want concise, direct answers. Which combination of changes most effectively controls response length?
options: Use a different embedding model — embedding quality determines response length in RAG applications
Increase the model size — larger models naturally produce more concise outputs due to better compression of knowledge

Add explicit length instructions in the system prompt ('Respond in 2-3 sentences maximum'), set the max_tokens parameter to limit output length, and optionally use a lower temperature to favor shorter, higher-probability continuations
Enable streaming responses so users see text as it's generated — this creates a perception of faster, more direct responses even if total length is the same
explain: Response length is controlled through: (1) Explicit prompt instructions — models follow instructions for length constraints reliably. (2) max_tokens — hard limit on output tokens, though setting this too low can truncate correct answers. (3) Temperature — lower temperatures reduce exploratory text. All three together provide layered control. Larger models generate longer, more detailed responses on average — the opposite of the desired effect. Streaming is a delivery mechanism and doesn't affect total content. Embedding models affect retrieval in RAG systems, not response length in generation.

q: A news organization uses a generative AI to assist journalists in writing articles. During review, an editor discovers that the AI-generated draft included a fabricated quote attributed to a real public figure. What responsible AI principle is violated and what process should prevent this?
options: The 'transparency' principle is violated — the article should disclose that AI assistance was used; add an AI disclosure statement to the article

The 'truthfulness and accuracy' principle is violated — generative AI must not attribute fabricated statements to real people; implement a mandatory human review process for all AI-generated content before publication, with specific checks for quotations and attributed statements
The 'copyright' principle is violated — the AI reproduced copyrighted content from its training data; implement a DMCA compliance check on all outputs
No principle is violated — generative AI is known to be creative and quotations from public figures are a normal creative output that readers understand may be approximate
explain: Attributing fabricated statements to real people violates the core principles of accuracy and non-deception — it constitutes misinformation and potential defamation. In journalism, this is a fundamental ethics violation. AI systems can confabulate plausible-sounding quotes that were never said. The process control is mandatory human editorial review with explicit verification of all attributed quotes against primary sources. Copyright is a separate issue. Transparency about AI use is also a principle but doesn't address the fabricated quote. Fabricated quotes attributed to real people are never acceptable — not a 'creative output.'

q: A company wants to understand the difference between 'generative AI' and 'discriminative AI' before deciding which to use for a text spam detection task. Which description is CORRECT?
options: Both are equivalent for spam detection — the choice should be based solely on vendor pricing
Discriminative AI generates content by discriminating between good and bad examples; generative AI discriminates between real and fake data to improve quality

Discriminative AI learns the boundary between classes (spam vs. not spam) and directly predicts probability of class membership — better for classification tasks like spam detection. Generative AI learns to generate text by modeling the full data distribution — better for content creation tasks
Generative AI is always better than discriminative AI because it understands the full distribution of data, making it more accurate for all tasks
explain: For spam detection (a binary classification task), discriminative models (logistic regression, BERT classifier, gradient boosting) are typically superior — they directly learn the decision boundary between spam and not-spam, are fast (single forward pass), cost-effective, and achieve high accuracy with labeled training data. Generative AI (GPT-style) could be prompted for spam classification but has higher latency, higher inference cost, and doesn't inherently outperform discriminative models on well-defined classification tasks. Generative models excel at generation (writing, translation, code). The choice should be driven by task type, not just vendor pricing.

q: A company implements a RAG system using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases. During testing, they notice that for questions requiring information from multiple documents, the answers are incomplete because only one document is retrieved. How should this be improved?
options: Switch from vector search to keyword-based BM25 search, which retrieves from multiple documents more effectively
Index documents by document rather than chunk — retrieve the top-1 most relevant full document rather than multiple chunks
Increase the chunk size so each chunk contains more information from a single document, reducing the need for cross-document retrieval

Increase the number of retrieved chunks (top-k) from 1-2 to 5-10, and implement a multi-hop retrieval strategy where the initial answer is used to formulate a second retrieval query to fetch complementary information
explain: Multi-document questions require: (1) Higher top-k retrieval to fetch relevant chunks from multiple documents simultaneously. (2) Multi-hop retrieval for questions that require sequential reasoning — first retrieve general context, use that to refine a second query, retrieve specific details. This enables chain-of-thought retrieval for complex questions. Larger chunk sizes include more information per chunk but don't help with cross-document synthesis — you still need chunks from multiple documents. BM25 keyword search doesn't inherently retrieve from more documents than vector search. Retrieving full documents instead of chunks increases context length dramatically and includes much irrelevant content.

q: A company's generative AI assistant answers customer questions about their products. The product team wants to know if the assistant's knowledge is current and accurate. They create a test set of 100 questions with verified correct answers. Which combination of metrics provides the MOST comprehensive quality assessment?
options: Cost per query as the primary metric — the lowest cost model that passes a minimum quality threshold should be selected

Accuracy (what percentage of answers are factually correct), hallucination rate (what percentage contain information not in the source documents), response latency (how fast answers are generated), and customer satisfaction score (from user ratings) — together these cover quality, reliability, speed, and user experience
BLEU score and ROUGE-L score, which provide objective automated measurement of text quality that correlates perfectly with human judgment
Model perplexity (how surprised the model is by the test questions) as an intrinsic measure of model quality
explain: Comprehensive quality assessment requires multiple dimensions: (1) Accuracy against verified answers measures factual correctness. (2) Hallucination rate measures fabricated content (not in source documents). (3) Latency measures operational performance (user experience). (4) Customer satisfaction from actual users captures dimensions automated metrics miss (helpfulness, tone, resolution). This multi-dimensional view covers quality, safety, performance, and user experience. BLEU/ROUGE measure text overlap — high BLEU doesn't equal correctness. Perplexity is an intrinsic model property, not a response quality metric. Cost optimization is important but shouldn't override quality thresholds.

q: A company is deciding whether to use Claude Haiku, Claude Sonnet, or Claude Opus via Amazon Bedrock for their document summarization pipeline. The pipeline processes 50,000 documents per day and the summaries are for internal use (not customer-facing). Cost is the primary concern while maintaining acceptable quality. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Use the same model for all documents to simplify operations — complexity of routing outweighs any cost savings
Split traffic equally between Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus, average the three outputs to get the best summary

Start with Claude Haiku (fastest and lowest cost), benchmark its summary quality against Claude Sonnet on a sample of 500 documents using human evaluation, and use Haiku if quality is acceptable — use Sonnet only for documents where Haiku quality is insufficient (routing by document complexity)
Use Claude Opus for all documents to guarantee the highest quality summaries, accepting the higher cost as necessary for business value
explain: For internal-use summaries where quality thresholds are more flexible, Claude Haiku (fastest, cheapest — ~20x less than Opus per token) should be evaluated first. A 500-document sample with human evaluation tests if Haiku quality meets the internal standard. If acceptable for most documents, route to Haiku as the default; use a complexity classifier to escalate complex documents to Sonnet. This tiered approach optimizes cost while maintaining quality. Using Opus for all 50,000 documents is expensive if Haiku works. Averaging three model outputs is complex, expensive, and produces incoherent blended summaries. Routing complexity is manageable compared to 20x cost differences.

q: A developer sends a 100-page PDF document (approximately 50,000 tokens) to Amazon Bedrock Claude Sonnet (200K context window) for analysis. The response is correct but the API call is slow and expensive. What technique reduces cost and latency for repeated questions about the same document?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock's prompt caching feature (where supported) to cache the document portion of the prompt — subsequent questions about the same document reuse the cached key-value computations, significantly reducing both cost (cached tokens are cheaper) and latency (no re-processing of the document)
Split the document into 10-page chunks and only send the relevant chunk for each question
Fine-tune the model on the document content so all document knowledge is embedded in weights and doesn't need to be sent with each request
Compress the PDF using gzip before sending to the API to reduce the token count
explain: Amazon Bedrock prompt caching (available on select models including Claude) allows the key-value (KV) attention cache to be reused for the static portion of the prompt (the document). The document is processed once and the cache is reused for subsequent questions — reducing cost for cached tokens (typically 90% discount) and latency (no re-attention over the 50,000 document tokens). This is ideal for repeated analysis of the same document. PDF compression doesn't reduce tokens (text extraction happens before tokenization). Splitting into chunks requires knowing which chunk contains the answer. Fine-tuning for a single document is completely impractical.

q: A company deploys Amazon Q Business for their employees. They connect it to their Salesforce CRM, SharePoint, and Confluence. An employee asks Q: 'What is the revenue forecast for Q3?' Q Business returns an answer but the employee is unsure which source the answer came from. What Amazon Q Business feature addresses this concern?
options: Use Amazon Q Business API to retrieve the raw documents and build a custom citation layer in the application

Amazon Q Business includes citations in its responses — each fact or claim is linked to the specific source document and passage from which it was retrieved, allowing employees to verify the answer and trace it to authoritative sources
Amazon Q Business generates answers from its internal knowledge without citing sources — employees must verify claims independently using the connected data sources
Enable Amazon Q Business's 'fact-check mode' in the settings to require the model to verify each claim before including it in responses
explain: Amazon Q Business automatically includes citations with each response — it shows which specific documents, pages, and passages were used to generate the answer. This is a built-in feature that enables employees to click through to the source document to verify claims. For a Q3 revenue forecast, the citation would link to the specific Salesforce report or spreadsheet used. There is no separate 'fact-check mode' to enable — citations are on by default. Amazon Q Business does have internal general knowledge but for enterprise data it retrieves from connected sources and cites them. Building a custom citation layer is unnecessary.

q: A company wants to ensure that confidential employee PII (names, social security numbers, salaries) stored in Amazon S3 is never sent to Amazon Bedrock foundation models for analysis. Developers sometimes inadvertently include full employee records in their prompts. Which control prevents this?
options: Create an IAM policy denying developers the s3:GetObject permission on the PII bucket so they cannot retrieve the data to include in prompts

Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with sensitive information filters for PII types (SSN, names, salary information) — Guardrails can detect and block or redact PII from both prompts (before sending to the model) and responses (before returning to users)
Enable Amazon Macie on the S3 buckets containing employee data to detect PII and send alerts when developers access these buckets
Encrypt the PII data with a KMS key that developers don't have access to — they will see ciphertext rather than PII when reading the bucket
explain: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with sensitive information filters operate in the request pipeline — they intercept prompts before they reach the foundation model and either block requests containing PII or redact the PII (replace with [REDACTED]). This directly prevents the PII from being processed by the model. Guardrails can detect SSN patterns, credit card numbers, email addresses, and other PII types. Macie detects PII in S3 but cannot prevent it from being sent to Bedrock after being retrieved. IAM denying S3 access prevents data retrieval but is too broad (developers need data for legitimate analysis). KMS encryption means developers see ciphertext — but they could still send raw PII from other sources.

q: A company builds a customer support system where an AI agent must handle complex multi-step workflows: (1) retrieve customer account info, (2) check order status, (3) determine eligibility for a refund, (4) process the refund if eligible, and (5) send a confirmation email. Which orchestration approach BEST handles this multi-step workflow?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock Agents with action groups mapped to each capability (account lookup, order status, refund eligibility check, refund processing, email sending) — the agent autonomously decides which actions to call based on conversation context and chains them together to complete the workflow
Build a hardcoded sequential Lambda function that always executes all 5 steps for every customer query, regardless of what the customer actually needs
Use Amazon SWF (Simple Workflow Service) to manually define the exact sequence of steps for all possible customer scenarios in a workflow definition
Let the foundation model call all actions in parallel and merge results — parallel execution is always faster than sequential
explain: Amazon Bedrock Agents use an agent loop (ReAct pattern) to autonomously plan and execute multi-step tasks. The agent evaluates the conversation, decides which action to call next based on previous results, and continues until the goal is achieved. This handles the conditional logic (check eligibility BEFORE processing refund) and variable workflow paths (some customers don't need refunds) without hardcoding all paths. Hardcoded sequential Lambda misses the conditional nature (refunds processed when not needed). SWF manual definitions cannot handle the open-ended conversational nature. Parallel execution breaks causal dependencies (can't check eligibility before getting order status).

q: A company asks: 'What is the fundamental difference between a foundation model and a traditional ML model?' Which answer is MOST accurate?
options: Foundation models are always better than traditional ML models for any task due to their size and training data

Foundation models are large pre-trained models (billions of parameters) trained on broad, diverse datasets that can be adapted to many tasks via prompting or fine-tuning; traditional ML models are task-specific, trained from scratch on curated labeled datasets for one specific task
Traditional ML models cannot process text; foundation models were invented to enable text processing in machine learning
Foundation models are cloud-only; traditional ML models run exclusively on-premises
explain: Foundation models (like GPT-4, Claude, Llama) are characterized by their enormous scale (billions of parameters), diverse pre-training data (text, code, images from the internet), and versatility — they can perform many tasks via prompting or fine-tuning without task-specific training. Traditional ML models (logistic regression, decision trees, neural networks for specific tasks) are trained from scratch on curated, labeled data for one specific task and have no inherent cross-task capability. Foundation models aren't universally better — for structured tabular prediction with abundant labeled data, traditional ML often wins. Text processing has existed long before foundation models (n-grams, SVMs, early NLP). Foundation models can run anywhere with sufficient compute.

q: A startup wants to give their Amazon Bedrock-powered AI assistant a consistent personality and specific behavioral guidelines (always polite, never discuss politics, respond in the user's language). Where should these instructions be placed for maximum effectiveness?
options: These cannot be specified via API — they require fine-tuning the model on examples demonstrating the desired personality and behavior
In the max_tokens parameter metadata field where the model reads additional instructions before generating
In the first user message of each conversation, repeated at the beginning of every session

In the system prompt — the system prompt establishes persistent context and instructions that apply to the entire conversation, including personality, behavioral rules, and constraints. It is processed with higher priority than user messages in most foundation models
explain: The system prompt (system role in the Converse API or system parameter in InvokeModel) is the correct location for persistent instructions, persona definition, and behavioral guidelines. It provides context before any user interaction and is treated with higher authority than user messages. Well-crafted system prompts reliably establish tone, language, and behavioral constraints. Repeating instructions in every user message is verbose, consumes user context budget, and is less effective than a dedicated system prompt. max_tokens is only a numeric parameter with no metadata. While fine-tuning can reinforce behaviors, system prompts are sufficient for personality and constraints and require no training.

q: A company evaluates foundation models for a customer service chatbot and measures two metrics: groundedness (answers match source documents) and coherence (answers are logically structured). Model A scores high on groundedness but low on coherence. Model B scores high on coherence but low on groundedness. Which model is preferable for a CUSTOMER-FACING support chatbot and why?
options: Model A (high groundedness) is preferable — for customer support, providing accurate information grounded in official documentation is more important than stylistic coherence; incoherent but correct answers can be improved with prompt engineering, while hallucinated but coherent answers cause real customer harm
Model B (high coherence) is preferable — customers care about clear communication; accuracy can be improved by adding more examples to the prompt
Both models are equally unsuitable — only a model scoring high on both metrics should be used for customer-facing applications
The choice should be based solely on cost per token — the cheaper model should be selected regardless of quality differences
explain: For customer-facing support applications, accuracy is paramount over stylistic quality. An inaccurate but well-written answer that contradicts official policy or provides wrong information causes customer harm, support escalations, and potential legal liability. Groundedness (factual accuracy to source documents) is the higher-priority metric. Coherence issues can often be addressed through prompt engineering (e.g., 'Format your response as: 1. Direct answer. 2. Explanation. 3. Next steps.'). A hallucinating but fluent model is dangerous. However, if both scores are unacceptably low, the application should not be deployed until a better model is found.

q: A developer is building an application that generates different creative content for each user request (poems, stories, marketing slogans). They want maximum creativity and diversity in outputs. Which parameter configuration encourages the MOST creative outputs?
options: High temperature (1.5+) alone — extremely high temperature maximizes creativity by forcing the model to always choose random tokens
Temperature=0 with top_p=1.0 — maximum probability tokens always produce the most refined and sophisticated outputs
Low temperature (0.1) with low top_p (0.1) — restrictive sampling forces the model to concentrate its creativity on the best possible tokens

High temperature (0.8-1.0) combined with high top_p (0.9-1.0) — together they broaden the token sampling distribution, allowing the model to explore less common but potentially creative word choices and narrative directions
explain: For creative generation, higher temperature (0.8-1.0) increases randomness in token selection, exploring a broader range of vocabulary and narrative directions. High top_p (nucleus sampling) ensures the model considers a wider portion of the probability distribution rather than being restricted to the top few tokens. Together, these parameters encourage diverse, surprising, and creative outputs. Temperature=0 produces the same deterministic output every time — the opposite of diversity. Extremely high temperature (>1.2) causes the model to select nearly random tokens, producing incoherent text rather than creative text. Low temperature with low top_p makes outputs highly conservative and repetitive.

q: A company builds a RAG system for a legal research assistant. Lawyers need answers that cite specific paragraphs and page numbers from source documents. Which approach ensures accurate, verifiable citations in RAG responses?
options: Use an internet search tool in an agent to find the original source after the model generates an answer, then append citations automatically
Ask the model to generate citations based on its training knowledge — large models have memorized many legal documents and can provide accurate citations
Include full document text in each API call so the model can generate page numbers by counting paragraphs in its input

Store document metadata (filename, page number, paragraph number) alongside each chunk in the vector store; retrieve chunks with their metadata; instruct the model to cite specific source metadata for every claim; validate that cited sources actually appear in the retrieved chunks before returning to the user
explain: Accurate citation in RAG requires: (1) Preserving document structure metadata (filename, page, paragraph) when chunking and indexing. (2) Returning this metadata alongside retrieved content. (3) Instructing the model to cite specific metadata for each claim. (4) Validating that cited sources are actually in the retrieved context (hallucinated citations are common when models are asked to cite without retrieved evidence). This architecture produces verifiable, accurate citations. Asking the model to cite from training data is unreliable — models confabulate citations (one of the most common hallucination types). Full document inclusion is expensive and doesn't guarantee accurate page numbering. Post-hoc internet search is slow and may find different versions of the document.

q: A company wants to fine-tune a Claude model on Amazon Bedrock using their proprietary customer interaction data. A data scientist asks: 'What format should our training data be in?' Which format is required for Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning?
options: Plain text format where each training example is separated by a special delimiter token and the model learns from unsupervised prediction
CSV format with columns: 'input', 'output', 'label' — standard tabular format that all Amazon Bedrock models accept
JSON format with a single large file containing all examples in an array — larger files train faster due to reduced I/O overhead

JSONL format with each line containing a complete training example in the instruction-response format: {'prompt': 'customer question', 'completion': 'ideal agent response'} or chat format with system/user/assistant turns depending on the model's fine-tuning specification
explain: Amazon Bedrock fine-tuning requires JSONL (JSON Lines) format — one JSON object per line. Each line represents a complete training example. For instruction-tuning, the format typically includes a 'prompt' and 'completion' field, or uses a messages array with system/user/assistant turns (similar to the chat format used for inference). The exact schema varies by model family. JSONL allows streaming large training files without loading the entire dataset into memory. CSV is not a supported fine-tuning format for Bedrock. Plain text unsupervised training would be continued pre-training, not instruction fine-tuning. A single large JSON array is not the JSONL format (each line must be independently parseable).
